# Appendix A. Panic and Safety Reference {#sec-panic .unnumbered}

The one place the book's DANGER callouts point back to. The shell has
no trash folder and no reflog: a destructive command takes effect the
moment you press Enter, and the only reliable recovery is the one you
set up *beforehand*. This appendix collects the destructive commands
in one table, then the recovery you actually have, situation by
situation. Each row and step names the chapter that teaches it in
full; [Chapter 7](chapters/07-pipes-redirection-danger.qmd) is the
book's dedicated danger chapter.

## Destructive commands at a glance {.unnumbered}

Every command below is destructive or security-sensitive, and most
cannot be undone. Read the guard column before you run any of them
from memory.

| Command | What it destroys | Guard first | Ch |
|---|---|---|---|
| `rm -rf <path>` | a file or whole tree, no undo | quote the path; `${VAR:?}`; delete a named dir, not a pattern | 7 |
| `rm -rf "$X"/` (X unset) | expands toward `/` or your whole disk | `set -u`; `${X:?}`; never put `rm -rf` beside an unguarded variable | 7 |
| `>` (redirect) | truncates the target to empty before writing | `set -o noclobber`; never redirect a command into its own input | 7 |
| `cp` / `mv` onto an existing file | replaces the destination silently | `ls` the destination first; `cp -i` / `mv -i` | 5 |
| `sed -i` on a file | overwrites in place; kills raw-data provenance | write to a new file with `>`; if truly in place, `-i.bak`; never on raw data | 8 |
| `find ... -delete` / `-exec rm {} +` | every match across the tree | run `-print` first, read the list, then swap in the delete | 9 |
| `chmod 777` / `chmod -R 777` | opens files to every account (security hole) | grant the minimum: `chmod u+rw`, `600`, `644` | 10 |
| `kill -9` (SIGKILL) | no cleanup; corrupt output, stale locks | try default `kill` (SIGTERM) first | 10 |
| secret on a command line | leaks to history, `ps`, logs, transcripts | read it from an env var or a `600` file | 10 |
| `rsync --delete` | destination files absent from the source | dry-run `-n`; read the `deleting` lines; mind the trailing slash | 14 |
| `make clean` | generated files via `rm -f` / `rm -rf` | read the recipe; keep paths inside the project | 12 |
| `curl` piped into `sh` | runs unread remote code as you | fetch, read, checksum, then run | 3, 7 |
| `git reset --hard` / `git clean -fd` | uncommitted work; untracked files | commit a clean baseline first; the companion Git book owns the undo | 17 |
| `git push --force` | rewrites shared remote history | use `--force-with-lease`; protect the branch; never on a shared `main` | 17 |

## When it has already happened {.unnumbered}

### You overwrote a file with `cp`, `mv`, or `>` {.unnumbered}

The original is not in a trash folder, so recovery depends entirely on
what you set up beforehand. If the file was committed, the companion
Git book's `git restore` brings it back, which is one more reason to
commit early and often. If it was a generated artifact, regenerate it:
on the asset-pricing example a clobbered `output/` file rebuilds from
the seeded scripts with one `make` ([Chapter 12](chapters/12-make-pipeline.qmd)),
which is the whole point of making outputs reproducible rather than
precious. For an untracked, unbacked-up raw file, there may be no
recovery at all. See [Chapter 5](chapters/05-navigation.qmd) and
[Chapter 7](chapters/07-pipes-redirection-danger.qmd).

### You ran a destructive `rm` (or `find -delete`) {.unnumbered}

Stop typing. The deleted data is not in a trash folder, but on a disk
the blocks are often still there until something overwrites them, so
the worst thing you can do is keep working in that directory. The
honest recovery is the one you set up first: a recent commit (for
files Git was tracking), a backup (Time Machine, `rsync` snapshots,
[Chapter 14](chapters/14-ssh-remote-compute.qmd)), or the seed-based
regeneration this book's example relies on, where `make` rebuilds a
deleted `output/` from code. For an untracked, unbacked-up raw file,
there may be no recovery at all, which is the entire argument for
treating `rm` with caution. See
[Chapter 7](chapters/07-pipes-redirection-danger.qmd) and
[Chapter 9](chapters/09-finding-things.qmd).

### A secret leaked (history, `ps`, a log, a screenshot, an agent) {.unnumbered}

Do not try to delete every copy; you can rarely find them all, and
deletion does nothing against anyone who already read it. The only
real fix is to *rotate* the credential: generate a new key or password
at the provider, update wherever the real service reads it, and revoke
the old one, which makes the leaked value worthless. Treat any secret
that has touched a command line, a screen, or a repository as
compromised. Then remove the file from Git's future (the companion Git
book covers purging it from history) and fix the habit that put it on
the command line. See
[Chapter 10](chapters/10-processes-permissions-secrets.qmd) and, for
agent prompts, [Chapter 17](chapters/17-ai-terminal.qmd).

### An `rsync --delete` erased the wrong files {.unnumbered}

`rsync --delete` makes the destination match the source by deleting
whatever the source lacks; there is no trash and no undo. Recovery is
the backup or commit you kept, same as `rm`. The prevention is the
dry-run you skipped: always `rsync -a -v -n --delete` first and read
the `deleting` lines before dropping `-n`. See
[Chapter 14](chapters/14-ssh-remote-compute.qmd).

### An AI agent ran something destructive {.unnumbered}

An agent in a trust-everything mode can run every command in the table
above, on its own reading of what you meant, faster than you can parse
its intent. The recovery is identical to doing it yourself: for a
tracked file, `git diff` and `git restore`; for an untracked one,
often nothing. The prevention is to keep a clean committed baseline
before you turn an agent loose, run it in auto-edit rather than
full-trust mode so edits arrive as reviewable diffs, and never run a
bypass mode on a machine holding data you cannot regenerate. See
[Chapter 17](chapters/17-ai-terminal.qmd).

## The defenses that actually work {.unnumbered}

Every recovery above reduces to something you did in advance. Build
these habits and most panics become a one-line rebuild instead of a
loss.

- **Commit early and often.** A tracked file is reviewable and
  reversible: `git restore` brings back its last committed state, and
  `git reflog` can recover a lost commit or branch tip (not an
  arbitrary edit you never committed). That is why you commit a clean
  baseline before risky work.
- **Make outputs regenerable, not precious.** Raw inputs and code are
  the only things worth protecting; `data/clean` and `output` are
  cache that `make` rebuilds from a seed
  ([Chapter 6](chapters/06-organizing-project.qmd),
  [Chapter 12](chapters/12-make-pipeline.qmd)).
- **Keep a backup of the irreplaceable.** Licensed or hand-collected
  raw data that no seed can regenerate needs a real backup
  (Time Machine, an `rsync` snapshot, a deposit) before you ever point
  a destructive command near it.
- **Look before you leap.** Run the read-only form on the *exact*
  pattern first: `ls` the destination, `find ... -print`,
  `rsync -n`, `make -n`. Then swap in the destructive action.
- **Guard the guard rails.** `set -o noclobber`, `cp -i` / `mv -i`,
  and `${VAR:?}` are one guard each, not the guard; a script that runs
  with them unset is unprotected, so write scripts that set their own
  ([Chapter 11](chapters/11-scripts-fail-loudly.qmd),
  [Chapter 18](chapters/18-startup-files-path.qmd)).

See the [reproducibility and safety checklist](#sec-checklist) for the
pass that ties these habits together, and the
[divergence reference](#sec-divergence) for where a command's
destructive behavior differs across macOS and Linux.
